articles.title: 4,000 quake victims in GenSan receive DSWD cash aid
articles.description: GENERAL SANTOS CITY — Around 4,000 quake-affected workers and residents received P5,000 cash assistance here through the Department of Social Welfare and Development’s (DSWD) Assistance to Individuals in Crisis Situation (AICS) program. Distribution of cash aid began on Wednesday, two days after Monday’s magnitude 7.8 earthquake, which disrupted work and business operations in the city and forced temporary closures of numerous establishments. Aside from P5,000 cash, the DSWD also provided family food packs (FFPs) to affected families as part of the relief response. READ: Mindanao earthquake: Marcos says kin of fatalities to receive cash aid The turnout included workers from malls, [...]... Keep on reading: 4,000 quake victims in GenSan receive DSWD cash aid

articles.title: Cebu gears up for rainy season, water impounding facilities, dams eyed
articles.description: CEBU CITY, Philippines — Cebu province is gearing up for the rainy season and the coming El Niño, which experts have described as one that could possibly become a “super El Niño.” This developed as Governor Pamela Baricuatro, during a press briefing on Wednesday, June 10, at the Capitol, outlined plans to ease or address the possible problems that the rainy season and the possible “super El Niño” could bring. Water impounding facilities and dams First on the list is building more water impounding facilities in watershed areas and constructing dams in the eight rivers in Cebu. READ: Cebu eyes [...]... articles.title: Over 3,000 Mindanao earthquake aftershocks recorded — Phivolcs
articles.description: MANILA, Philippines — A total of 3,019 aftershocks have so far been recorded following the magnitude 7.8 earthquake that shook southern Mindanao earlier this week, according to the Philippine Institute of Volcanology and Seismology (Phivolcs). In its latest bulletin sent to reporters, as of 11 a.m. on June 11, Phivolcs said the aftershocks ranged between magnitudes 1.2 and 6.4. Of the 3,019 aftershocks recorded so far, 790 of them were plotted and 61 were felt. READ: Mindanao earthquake death toll climbs to 47 The original magnitude 7.8 earthquake struck 32 kilometers (km) southwest of Maasim, Sarangani at 7:37 a.m. on [...]... Keep on reading: Over 3,000 Mindanao earthquake aftershocks recorded — Phivolcs

articles.title: India ‘actively working’ to deprive Pakistan of water
articles.description: NEW DELHI: India is working to ensure “not a single drop of water” will flow into neighbouring Pakistan, the water minister has said, after New Delhi put the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) into abeyance following the Pahalgam attack in April 2025. “It is certain, not a single drop of water will go (to Pakistan) in the coming years,” Minister of Water CR Patil told India’s ANI news agency late Tuesday. Patil, speaking in Hindi, said that India is “actively working on it” after “directives” from Prime Minister Narendra Modi. The treaty governs the use of water from six rivers, whose headwaters originate in India but flow into Pakistan as part of the Indus basin — a resource relied on by hundreds of millions. The Indus cuts through ultra-sensitive demarcation lines between India and Pakistan in contested, Muslim-majority Kashmir — a Himalayan territory both countries claim in full. Indian water minister says working to ensure ‘not a single drop of water’ flows downstream India in May 2025 suspended its IWT membership after accusing Islamabad of backing a deadly attack on tourists on the Indian side of Kashmir—charges Pakistan denied. The nuclear-armed neighbours fought a four-day conflict —with intense drone, missile and artillery exchanges, killing nearly 70 people on both sides. The issue of water has remained a bitter point of contention since. Experts say that India’s existing dams do not have the capacity to block or divert water, and can only regulate timings of when it releases flows. Cutting flows would have serious implications for Pakistan’s agriculture and overall economy, but any project would take several years before it has an impact. An official in the Indian-held Kashmir said that any work would “not be possible to start before mid-2027”, and would take at least five years to complete. Pakistan has previously said it would consider any attempt to change the flow of cross-border waterways as an “act of war”, saying the 1960 treaty remained in force as there was no mechanism to unilaterally withdraw from it. It had also urged the UN Security Council to take up the dispute over the IWT, warning that India’s continued unilateral suspension of the treaty carries “grave peace and security, and humanitarian consequences” for South Asia. Published in Dawn, June 11th, 2026
